cases.title: Needlestick After an IV
cases.presentation: While starting an IV on a bleeding trauma patient of unknown history, you sustain a needlestick to your finger. The patient's infection status is unknown.
cases.question: What is the correct immediate sequence of actions?
options: Ignore it and finish the shift
Wash the site with soap and water, report the exposure per protocol, and seek prompt evaluation including consideration of HIV PEP
Apply a tourniquet to your arm
Wait several days to see if symptoms develop
cases.explanation: A needlestick is a true bloodborne exposure. Immediately wash the site with soap and water, report per protocol, and seek prompt evaluation. HIV post-exposure prophylaxis is time-sensitive and most effective when started as soon as possible; hepatitis B status and testing are addressed based on your vaccination status.
cases.clinicalPearl: Wash, report, evaluate—and remember HIV PEP works best within hours. Never sit on an exposure.

cases.title: Choosing the Right Precautions
cases.presentation: You respond to a patient with several weeks of cough, fever, night sweats, and weight loss who lives in a shelter. He is coughing frequently in the back of your ambulance.
cases.question: Which precautions are most appropriate?
options: Airborne precautions—fit-tested N95 for you and a surgical mask on the patient—because of suspected tuberculosis
cases.explanation: Chronic cough with fever, night sweats, and weight loss in a high-risk setting suggests tuberculosis, which is spread by the airborne route. Don a fit-tested N95 respirator, place a surgical mask on the patient, and ventilate the patient compartment—standard precautions alone are insufficient.
cases.clinicalPearl: Match the precaution to the route: suspected TB means airborne precautions—N95 for you, surgical mask for the patient.

cases.title: Splash Risk During Active Hemorrhage
cases.presentation: You are managing a patient with a spurting arterial wound and active airway bleeding. There is significant potential for blood splash to your face and eyes.
cases.question: What PPE level is appropriate?
options: Gloves plus face/eye protection and a gown, because splashing of blood and OPIM is likely
No PPE is needed if you work quickly
cases.explanation: When splashing of blood or other potentially infectious materials is likely, standard precautions escalate to include face/eye protection and a gown in addition to gloves and hand hygiene. Active arterial and airway bleeding clearly warrant full barrier protection.
cases.clinicalPearl: Standard precautions scale to the risk—when blood can splash, protect your eyes, face, and body, not just your hands.
